name: hs1_rc1_direct_query_rgb24
text: Surface note: "Holographic Surface stores SEAM-RC/1 as exact pixel bytes." Direct queries read the embedded machine language without OCR or SQLite import.
queries: "Holographic Surface stores SEAM-RC/1 as exact pixel bytes."

name: hs1_mirl_direct_search_bw1
dsl:
entity project "SEAM" as p1
claim c1:
  subject p1
  predicate holographic_surface
  object direct_read_memory
claim c2:

name: hs1_rc1_direct_query_rgba32
text: Surface density note: "RGBA32 stores four exact channel bytes per pixel." SEAM keeps RGB24 as the default and uses RGBA32 only when explicitly requested.
queries: "RGBA32 stores four exact channel bytes per pixel."

name: hs1_rc1_direct_query_rgba64
text: Surface density note: "RGBA64 stores eight exact channel bytes per pixel." SEAM uses RGBA64 only when 16-bit channel density is explicitly requested.
queries: "RGBA64 stores eight exact channel bytes per pixel."

name: hs1_rc1_richer_heading_recall_md
text:
## Production Loop

The SEAM visual-memory loop compiles documents into directly readable machine language, encodes that machine language into a lossless PNG surface, stores the surface in an addressable library, and answers queries by reading pixel bytes back into MIRL or SEAM-RC/1 records.

## Why This Matters

Direct surface query removes the OCR and SQLite-import dependency for already-compiled memory.

name: hs1_rc1_richer_table_cell_lookup_md
text:
Benchmark snapshot for the SEAM visual-memory loop:

| metric | value |
| --- | --- |
| surface_exact_rate | 1.0 |
| payload_hash_match_rate | 1.0 |
| direct_query_exactness_rate | 1.0 |
| stored_query_exactness_rate | 1.0 |

All release-blocking gates remain at 100 percent for the surface family.

name: hs1_rc1_richer_citation_extraction
text:
According to Smith (2024) [Smith2024], holographic memory surfaces preserve direct readability of embedded machine language without OCR. SEAM extends this idea by binding MIRL or SEAM-RC/1 records to lossless PNG pixel bytes.

References:
[Smith2024] Smith, J. (2024). Holographic Memory in AI Systems. Journal of Memory Research 12(3), 45-62.
